clean_story: Buried danger.  Silent pressure triggers. "Mine" comes from the Latin mina, meaning "threat"—fitting for a device that waits unseen until weight betrays its presence. Most think mines explode on contact. Truth is, they detect pressure thresholds—like a scale set to ignore a rabbit but snap under a soldier’s boot. Inside, a spring-loaded firing pin rests against a compressed air chamber or a fragile glass vial. Step on it, and the weight pushes a plunger. Too light?—Nothing happens. Just right?—The pin shatters the vial, releasing a hammer to strike the detonator. Modern anti-tank mines use magnetic influence fuses—sensing metal mass above, not just weight. A car’s steel frame warps the mine’s magnetic field, closing an electric circuit. No pressure needed. Some mines even "count" pressure pulses—ignoring the first footstep but exploding on the second. Three related subjects are landmine clearance robots, how animals detect vibrations, and pressure-sensitive medical implants.
